coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots and their application as fluorescent probes for rutin detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Chunxiao
Zhang, Meng
Zheng, Na
Liu, Benhua
Tang, Ju
Tang, Jianing
Zhang, Fan
Chen, Guo
coredata.dc:description: Rutin has antioxidant, antitumor, antibacterial, antiviral, anti-aging, and analgesic effects, and it is a drug used to treat various circulatory disorders. However, rutin overdosage can seriously harm the health. The rutin detection method has drawbacks, including expensive costs, low sensitivity, and complicated steps. Therefore, given that carbon dots have superior fluorescence characteristics, the development of nanofluorescent probes for rutin detection is the research motivation of this article. N-CDs and N, B-CDs were produced by a hydrothermal approach using o-phenylenediamine, glucose, urea, and boron oxide, commonly available in the laboratory. The morphology and composition of CDs have been analyzed. According to the findings, the surfaces of the N-CDs and N, B-CDs were abundant in functional groups that contained oxygen, and they were spherical, uniform in size, and well distributed, which is beneficial for enhancing its fluorescence response to the detection object. In addition, two kinds of CDs emit bright yellow and blue-green fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm. Rutin was detected in solution using synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs as fluorescent probes. Rutin was discovered to have a fluorescence quenching effect on N-CDs and N, B-CDs. Within a specific concentration range, the N-CDs and N, B-CDs fluorescence intensities demonstrated an excellent linear relationship with the rutin concentration, and the corresponding correlation coefficients R2 were 0.9897 and 0.9409, respectively. To verify the detection accuracy of the experiment, we used the random forest algorithm to predict the detection model. The results showed that the experimental and predicted values were coincident, suggesting the possibility of using N-CDs and N, B-CDs as fluorescent probes to detect rutin. This work offers experimental procedures and data references to create doping CDs with superior fluorescence qualities. It broadens the use of doped CDs in the detection of rutin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doped carbon dots
Green synthesis
Rutin
Random forest model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity assessment of rutin to CDs
Analysis of optical properties of CDs
Fluorescence response of CDs to rutin
Random forest regression model predicts detection accuracy
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by rutin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart for the preparation of N-CDs and N, B-CDs.

TEM images of N-CDs (a), N, B-CDs (d) dispersed in pure water. (b) and (e) are the diameter distributions of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, respectively, and the blue line is the Gaussian fitting curve. (c) and (f) are the HRTEM images of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, respectively.
XRD spectra of N-CDs (a), XRD spectra of N, B-CDs (b), Zeta potentials of N-CDs and N, B-CDs (c), Changes in fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N,B-CDs(d).

(a), (b), and (c) show the full-scan XPS photoelectron spectra, C1s high-resolution spectra, and N1s high-resolution spectra of N-CDs, respectively. (d) are the infrared spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs (a for N-CDs, b for N, B-CDs). (e), (f), (g), and (h) are full-scan XPS photoelectron spectra, C1s high-resolution spectra, N1s high-resolution spectra, and B1s high-resolution spectra of N-CDs, respectively.
Raman spectra of N-CDs (a) and N, B-CDs (b), respectively.

(a) and (b) show the PL spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, respectively. The insets show chromaticity maps (top inset) and digital photographs (bottom inset) of N-CDs and N, B-CDs under 470 nm and 390 nm irradiation.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs + Rutin (a), UV–Vis absorption spectra of N, B-CDs and N, B-CDs + Rutin (b). Tauc curves for N-CDs and N-CDs + Rutin (c), and Tuac curves for N, B-CDs and N, B-CDs + Rutin (d).

PL emission spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs at different rutin solution concentrations (a) (c), respectively. PL intensity versus rutin solution concentration (b) (d), respectively. Ultraviolet image of N-CDs and N, B-CDs at different rutin solution concentrations (c) (f).

(a) and (b) show the degree of quenching of the fluorescence of N-CDs and N, B-CDs by different substances; (c) and (d) show the degree of quenching of the fluorescence of N-CDs and N, B-CDs by different substances coexisting with rutin, respectively.
Degree of quenching of N-CDs and N, B-CDs fluorescence by real samples.
Flow chart of random forest (a), Schematic diagram (b) of random forest.
Graph of N-CDs after smoothing (a). Matrix of inter-sample correlation coefficients (b). Feature importance (c). Random forest regression results (d).
Graph of N, B-CDs after smoothing (a). Matrix of inter-sample correlation coefficients (b). Feature importance (c). Random Forest regression results (d).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of rutin in the presence or absence of N-CDs and N, B-CDs (a). Schematic of fluorescence of N-CDs (yellow) and N, B-CDs (blue-green) under UV light (b). Diagram of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs and N, B-CDs (c). Schematic diagram of electron energy levels and electron transition channels of N-CDs(d). Schematic diagram of N-CDs and N, B-CDs after fluorescence quenching (e). Schematic diagram of electron energy levels and electron transition channels of N, B-CDs(f).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots and their application as fluorescent probes for rutin detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kunming Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rutin has antioxidant, antitumor, antibacterial, antiviral, anti-aging, and analgesic effects, and it is a drug used to treat various circulatory disorders. However, rutin overdosage can seriously harm the health. The rutin detection method has drawbacks, including expensive costs, low sensitivity, and complicated steps. Therefore, given that carbon dots have superior fluorescence characteristics, the development of nanofluorescent probes for rutin detection is the research motivation of this article. N-CDs and N, B-CDs were produced by a hydrothermal approach using o-phenylenediamine, glucose, urea, and boron oxide, commonly available in the laboratory. The morphology and composition of CDs have been analyzed. According to the findings, the surfaces of the N-CDs and N, B-CDs were abundant in functional groups that contained oxygen, and they were spherical, uniform in size, and well distributed, which is beneficial for enhancing its fluorescence response to the detection object. In addition, two kinds of CDs emit bright yellow and blue-green fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm. Rutin was detected in solution using synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs as fluorescent probes. Rutin was discovered to have a fluorescence quenching effect on N-CDs and N, B-CDs. Within a specific concentration range, the N-CDs and N, B-CDs fluorescence intensities demonstrated an excellent linear relationship with the rutin concentration, and the corresponding correlation coefficients R2 were 0.9897 and 0.9409, respectively. To verify the detection accuracy of the experiment, we used the random forest algorithm to predict the detection model. The results showed that the experimental and predicted values were coincident, suggesting the possibility of using N-CDs and N, B-CDs as fluorescent probes to detect rutin. This work offers experimental procedures and data references to create doping CDs with superior fluorescence qualities. It broadens the use of doped CDs in the detection of rutin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In 2004, Xu et al. discovered carbon nanodots with fluorescent properties while separating monolayer carbon nanotubes. Two years later, Sun et al. synthesized stabilized luminescent carbon nanoparticles of different sizes and named them “carbon quantum dots (CDs)” for the first time [1,2]. CDs are eco-friendly carbon nanomaterials with dimensions smaller than 10 nm [3]. In contrast to traditional metal-based semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have many properties, such as a comprehensive and tunable emission wavelength, excellent fluorescence properties, stable physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, low biotoxicity, high water solubility, ease of fabrication, ease of surface functionalization, and long excitation range [4–7]. Furthermore, CDs offer better optical properties compared to current organic fluorescent dyes, and the preparation process usually does not use hazardous metal elements, reducing environmental pollution [8–11]. Due to these excellent properties, CDs have been shown to have applications in energy, batteries, catalysis, biosensing, cellular imaging, fluorescent inks, medical diagnostics, etc [12–15]. However, in the early stages of carbon dots research, CDs' reported fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) are typically low, which limits their practical application.

Prior researchers have conducted numerous studies to increase the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. Two methods mainly modulate the fluorescence properties of CDs: one is to choose a suitable and optimal synthesis method to prepare CDs, and the other is to dope CDs with heteroatoms [15–17]. Acid oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, hydrothermal process, laser ablation, thermal injection, and microwave pyrolysis are the standard synthesis techniques used to prepare CDs [18–20]. The hydrothermal approach is most frequently employed because of its easy-to-use, inexpensive, and practical synthesis [21,22]. Kirubanithy et al. [23] produced fluorescent carbon quantum dots (FCQDs) with good water solubility by employing a hydrothermal process and coconut shells as a raw material. The results showed that FCQDs emit bright blue fluorescence with a wide optical band gap. Because of their selectivity for Fe3+, the FCDQs were applied to detecting Fe3+. Using p-phenylenediamine as a basic material, Shi et al. [24] prepared red-emitting CDs hydrothermally. Experiments have shown that CDs have high fluorescence intensity and help discriminate between normal and cancer cells. It has also been demonstrated that heteroatom doping is a popular technique for raising the yield of CDs [25].

Furthermore, Heteroatom doping is considered a promising approach to control the properties of carbon nanodots, especially their fluorescence and antioxidant activities [26,27]. It is worth mentioning that boron (B), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) elements have been validated and are suitable for doping various types of CDs. Typically, Lai et al. [28] prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots and found that the carbon dots possessed superior photoluminescence performances. Moreover, the N-CQDs could detect Fe3+ within a specific concentration range. Hua et al. [29] prepared nitrogen-fluorine co-doped CDs (N, F-CDs) with a 52.2 % high fluorescence quantum yield; it was discovered that N, F-CDs might be used for cell biomarking.

Rutin is a naturally occurring brassinaceous derivative considered one of the most biologically active flavonoids [30]. Numerous biological activities of rutin include its ability to scavenge free radicals and act as an antioxidant, antitumor, antibacterial, antiviral, anti-aging, and analgesic [31,32]. Many health foods and various beverages contain rutin [33]. It also treats several circulatory disorders by lowering blood pressure and capillary permeability [34]. However, the overdose of rutin can cause irreversible side effects in the body, such as headache, rash, acute liver and kidney damage, nervousness, and vision loss [35]. Therefore, the quantitative detection of rutin in drugs, health foods, and beverages is of great significance, improving the efficiency of rutin intake and also playing a role in ensuring its safety. Currently, capillary electrophoresis, cyclic voltage testing, and electrochemical detection are the most often used techniques for rutin detection [34,35]. However, these detection techniques come with some drawbacks, including high cost, low sensitivity, and complicated procedures; they also demand a high level of equipment design and inspection requirements and professional training [36]. Compared with other techniques, high sensitivity, quick response times, ease of use, and other attributes are present in fluor spectrometry [37].

This research used dextran, o-phenylenediamine, urea, and boron oxide as the raw materials to create blue-green emitting nitrogen-boron-doped carbon dots (N, B-CDs) and yellow-emitting nitrogen-boron-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using a quick and easy hydrothermal process, the synthetic carbon dots wereanalyzed using a range of characterization techniques, including morphology, composition,structure. The effect of different elemental doping on the photophysical performance of CDs was also explored. Furthermore, we found that N-CDs and N,B-CDs exhibit fluorescence response to rutin in solution. By establishing a rutin fluorescence sensor, we looked into the mechanism for quenching fluorescence and increased the range of valuable uses for them in the detection of rutin.

The chemical reagents used in the experiments, including glucose, urea, boron trioxide, o-phenylenediamine, ascorbic acid, glucose, sucrose, aspartic acid, cysteine, histidine, and rutin, were purchased from Shanghai Mellin Biochemistry Co Ltd (China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and did not require further purification for use. In addition, the sophora japonica extract powder used for the assay was purchased from Tongren Pharmacy, Nanjing, China.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Japan, JEOL JEM-F200) was used to investigate the morphology and microstructure of samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests of N-CDs and N, B-CDs were carried out using an X-ray diffractometer (D8 advance A25x, Bruker, Germany) with λ = 1.54056 nm, 40 kV, 200 mA, and a scanning range of 2θ = 5–90°, to analyze the N-CDs and N, B-CDs crystalline phases. Raman spectra of CDs were obtained utilizing a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer (Japan) to explore the chemical state of carbon in N-CDs and N, B-CDs. The elemental compositions of CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The surface functional groups of CDs were analyzed using FT-IR (Nicolet, IS10, USA), with the spectrum range being investigated to 400–4000 cm−1. An F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) was used to measure the room temperature fluorescence spectra of CDs. The absorption spectra were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Two kinds of CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal process. 0.216 g of o-phenylenediamine and 0.991 g of glucose, 0.300 g of urea, 0.348 g of boron oxide were accurately weighed into a 50 mL autoclavable reactor (lined with polytetrafluoroethylene). The reaction system was sealed by adding purified water to dissolve completely and heating for nine hours at 180 °C. CDs solutions were obtained by rapidly centrifuging the reaction products. In Fig. 1 , the experimental protocol is displayed.

Accurately weigh 0.1222 g of rutin powder in a beaker, add an appropriate amount of purified water to dissolve and transfer to a volume of 100 mL, and continue to add purified water to determine the volume. You can get a concentration of 2.0 × 10−6 mol/mL of rutin solution. This solution was diluted into a series of (0.1 × 10−6–2.0 × 10−6) concentration gradients and sealed and kept in reserve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity assessment of rutin to CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The same volume of N-CDs, N, B-CDs, and rutin solution with different concentrations (0.1 × 10−6–2.0 × 10−6) were placed in sample bottles, and the PL spectra were determined.

The size distribution histograms for carbon dots distributed in aqueous solution are displayed alongside the TEM images in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2(a) and (d), two kinds of CDs were well dispersed in aqueous solution with uniform sizes, most spherical. At the same time, a small portion showed irregular shapes, which could be attributed to the fact that the centrifuge technique used to prepare the samples was inefficient in separating the particles [38]. By contrasting the TEM images of samples, it is discovered that the doping of boron elements resulted in a more significant number of synthesized CDs with a more uniform distribution. The N-CDs diameters were mainly spread within the scope of 4–8 nm with a mean size of 6.05 nm, as depicted in Fig. 2(b) and (e). 5.66 nm mean size of N, B-CDs and are primarily spread in the 3–8 nm region in terms of diameter. The HRTEM images of N-CDs and N, B-CDs are displayed in Fig. 2(c) and (f), respectively. These images demonstrate that the N-CDs and N, B-CDs exhibit distinct lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.356 nm and 0.190 nm, respectively, which implies that the degree of crystallinity is high in CDs [39].

The crystalline or amorphous states of the CDs were detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern of N-CDs (Fig. 3 (a)) shows a broad diffraction peak at 27.85° and 40.88°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of the sp 2-hybridized graphitic carbons, respectively. The XRD spectra of N, B-CDs (Fig. 3(b)) show a broad diffraction peak at 28.11° and 40.17°, which also corresponds to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of sp 2 hybridized graphitic carbon. This outcome suggests that the synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs are graphitic structures [40,41]. From Fig. 3(c), it can be seen that the surfaces of N-CDs and N, B-CDs are mainly negatively charged, with zeta potentials of −13.19 mV and −1.46 mV, respectively. The higher the absolute value of the zeta potential is, the higher the surface charge is, and the stronger the electrostatic repulsion is between the particles, which usually reduces the agglomerations and improves dispersibility and stability. It can be seen that the stability of N-CDs is better compared to that of N, B-CDs, and the better stability is more favorable for the application of CDs in complex environments [42,43].

The fluorescence lifetime of a carbon dot is the average residence time when it is excited, reflecting the speed of the excited state energy release during the photoluminescence process [44]. This parameter is closely related to the electronic structure, surface state, doping elements, and surrounding environment of carbon dots. In order to further investigate the effects of different elemental doping on the optical properties of carbon dots, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N, B-CDs were tested separately, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 (d). As can be seen from the figure, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs is 1.28 ns, while that of the N, B-CDs is shortened to 1.08 ns. This difference arises from the modulation of the internal electronic structure and surface states of the carbon dots by the doping elements. Nitrogen doping may introduce more defects or change the electronic structure of the carbon dots, resulting in longer fluorescence lifetimes, and boron doping may instead change the energy band structure of the material, resulting in shorter lifetimes of the excited states [45].

The chemical compositions, surface states, and bonding of CDs were explored and analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The N-CDs primarily contain the elements C, N, and O, as seen in Fig. 4 (a) and (e). The atomic percentages of C1s, O1s, and N1s are 47.86 %, 43.91 %, and 8.23 %, respectively. The N, B-CDs mainly contain four elements, C, N, O, and B, and the atomic percentages of C1s, O1s, N1s, and B1s are 57.83 %, 33.44 %, 8.41 %, and 0.32 %, respectively. The high-resolution energy spectra of the C1s and N1s of N-CDs are displayed in Fig. 4(b) and (c), respectively. Three distinct peaks can be observed in the high-resolution energy spectra of C1s at 284.80 eV, 286.88 eV, and 288.88 eV, respectively. These peaks correspond to the C–C/C=C, C–O, and C=O groups [46,47]. Two distinct peaks at 397.98 eV and 399.58 eV, respectively, can be observed in the high-resolution energy spectra of N1s. These peaks correspond to pyrrole nitrogen (N–C–N) and an amine group (C–NH2) [48,49]. The high-resolution energy spectra of the C1s, N1s, and B1s of the N, B-CDs are displayed in Fig. 4(f), (g), and (h), respectively. Three distinct peaks were identified at 284.80 eV, 286.18 eV, and 288.68 eV in the C1s energy spectra of N, B-CDs. These peaks were associated with the C–C/C=C, C–O, and C=O groups, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 400.18 eV and 399.18 eV in the high-resolution energy spectra of N1s were linked to the graphitic nitrogen (N–C3) and the amine group (C–NH2) [50]. The high-resolution energy spectrum of B1s shows a characteristic peak at 187.98 eV, which is related to the B–O group. According to the analysis above, N-CDs and N, B-CDs have amine, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups on their surface. This indicates that element N was effectively doped into N-CDs and N, B-CDs, and element B was also effectively doped into N, B-CDs. Because of many hydrophilic groups, the water solubility of N-CDs and N, B-CDs was good.

Fig. 4(d) displays the FT-IR pattern of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, from which it can be seen that the absorption band at 3447.12 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH, the absorption peaks at 560.17 and 2931.89 cm−1 are both from the bending vibration of C–H. The characteristic absorption vibrational bands of C–C/C=C and C–O are located near 1595.77 and 1063.48 cm−1, respectively, and the absorption bands at 2356.94 and 1352.39 cm−1 correspond to the N–C3 and C–N/B–O stretching vibrations, successfully doping nitrogen and boron atoms into N-CDs and N, B-CDs [51]. The absorption peak at 2022.21 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C=O, which suggests that the synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs are oxygen-rich [36,52]. The analytical outcomes of the FT-IR spectra correspond to the above-mentioned results of XPS correspond to the above XPS results.

Fig. 5 (a) and (b) show the Raman spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, respectively. Graphitic carbon edge flaws, disorder, and planar crystal dimensions can be identified with the help of Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs have different absorption bands at 1323.08 cm−1, 1406.85 cm−1 (disordered D-band), and 1591.63 cm−1, 1606.22 cm−1 (G band) with different absorption bands. The G band denotes the sp 2 hybridization of C atoms in planar geometry, while the D band indicates the existence of dangling C atoms with sp 3 hybridization [41]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) between the disordered D-band and the crystalline G-band was 0.76 for N-CDs and 0.89 for N, B-CDs, and the difference in the values can be due to the disturbance of the carbon source structure of CDs caused by the N and B doping during the synthesis process. The carbon source analysis reveals that N-doped CDs (N-CDs) and N, B-co-doped CDs (N, B-CDs) exhibit significantly higher content of disordered carbon structural domains compared to undoped counterparts. This phenomenon is attributed to the lattice distortion induced by heteroatom doping (N for N-CDs; N/B synergistic effects for N, B-CDs), thereby generating abundant surface defect sites in both materials. In addition, N-CDs and N, B-CDs have broad half-width peaks (FWHM), as shown in Fig. 4, suggesting that they have more surface emission sites [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the PL spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, respectively. The figure illustrates that the N-CDs and N, B-CDs had excitation wavelengths between 420–480 nm and 320–390 nm, respectively. The positions of the PL emission peaks were noted at 543 nm and 454 nm, respectively, and the strongest PL was observed at 390 and 470 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. The emission peak positions of N-CDs have no dependence on the excitation wavelength and do not change with the excitation wavelength, which may be because of the surface passivation of N-CDs [7]. However, the emission peak positions of N, B-CDs have a weak dependence on the excitation wavelength, which is determined by the surface state of N, B-CDs. The chromaticity diagram shows the color coordinate positions of the N-CDs and N, B-CDs fluorophores, accurately representing the luminescence colors. The PL emission wavelengths of the N-CDs are located in the yellow band, and the PL emission wavelengths of the N, B-CDs are located in the blue-green band. In addition, we can also observe that under visible light, N-CDs and N, B-CDs are brown and light yellow, respectively, and both of them emit yellow fluorescence and blue-green fluorescence under UV light, respectively. The fluorescence analysis shows that the preparation of N-CDs and N, B-CDs is relatively successful, and the fluorescence intensity is relatively high.

Fig. 7 (a) and (b) display the UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs + Rutin, N, B-CDs, and N, B-CDs + Rutin, respectively. It is evident that when the Rutin reagent was added, the absorption peaks all showed a weak blue shift, and the absorption intensities were all weakened to some extent. N-CDs, N, B-CDs, N-CDs + Rutin, and N, B-CDs + Rutin showed absorption peaks at 300 and 419, 285, 286, and 283 nm, respectively. The absorption peaks at 300 and 419 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of C–C/C=C bonds in the sp 2 carbon region of the N-CDs system as well as the n-π* transition of the C=O or C-N groups, absorption peaks at 285, 286, and 283 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of the C–C/C=C bond [54,55]. It was also found that the absorption peak at 419 nm disappeared when the Rutin reagent was added, suggesting that the addition of the Rutin reagent altered the electronic properties of the surface/edge of the N-CDs, thereby inhibiting the fluorescence emission of N-CDs + Rutin [56]. The bandgap E g of CDs is calculated using the Tauc plot method(Fig. 7(c) and (d)). Band gaps of 2.45 eV and 3.93 eV, 3.65 eV, 4.01 eV and 3.79 eV can be noticed for N-CDs, N, B-CDs, N-CDs + rutin and N, B-CDs + rutin respectively, demonstrating that the band gaps of the N-CDs and N, B-CDs were successfully adjusted by the addition of the rutin reagent, which affected the fluorescence emission of the N-CDs and N, B-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to rutin
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 (a) and (c) show the PL spectra of N-CDs and N, B-CDs under different rutin solution concentrations; the corresponding excitation wavelengths of N-CDs and N, B-CDs are 470 nm and 390 nm, respectively. The association between the PL intensity and the rutin solution concentration is depicted in Fig. 8(b) and (d), respectively. The figures demonstrate that the addition of rutin solution resulted in a declining trend of the mixed system's fluorescence intensity. As the concentration of rutin solution increased, the fluorescence intensity progressively dropped, which indicated that the rutin solution produced fluorescence quenching of synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs.

The variation of fluorescence intensity in relation to the concentration of rutin solution and the scope of action is an essential index for evaluating the performance of fluorescent probes, so the peak values of the mixture at various rutin concentrations were counted. These values were plotted and linearly fitted(Fig. 8(b) and (d)). Ascan be indicated, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs and N, B-CDs had an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of rutin in the concentration ranges of 0–1.6 × 10−6mol/mL, 0–1.4 × 10−6mol/mL, respectively. The correlation coefficients, R2 were 0.9897 and 0.9409, respectively, and the R2 illustrated that the fitted curves were in high agreement with the test results.

In order to test the effect of rutin on the fluorescence properties of CDs, organic small molecules such as ascorbic acid (AC), citric acid (CA), glucose (Glu), sucrose (Sug), aspartic acid (Asp), dopamine (DA), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), and rutin (Rut) were selected to validate the selectivity of the fluorescent probes for CDs. The quenching effect of each substance is shown in Fig. 9 shows. The concentration of each detected substance in the system was the same in terms of F/F0. As shown in Fig. 9(a) and (b), rutin had a noticeable quenching effect on the fluorescence of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, with F/F0 of 0.35 and 0.34, respectively. In contrast, the other substances had less impact on the fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, rutin has high selectivity for the detection of N-CDs and N, B-CDs. To the N-CDs and N, B-CDs were added the mixed solutions of rutin with AC, CA, Glu, Sug, Asp, DA, His, and Cys, respectively, to verify the anti-interference of rutin to N-CDs and N, B-CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 9(c) and (d). As can be seen from the figures, when coexisting with other solutions, N-CDs, and N, B-CDs have the same evident fluorescence quenching effect on rutin. The F/F0 value after quenching is almost unaffected by the coexisting solutions, indicating that N-CDs and N, B-CDs have a strong resistance to the interference of other substances for the detection of rutin.

In addition, a quantity of acacia rice powder (later replaced by “sample”) was purchased to supplement the actual sample for rutin detection. The fluorescence intensity was measured by adding equal volumes of the sample solution to the N-CDs and N, B-CDs solutions, and F/F0 also calculated the fluorescence quenching effect. The results are shown in Fig. 10 . As can be seen from the figure, both N-CDs and N, B-CDs solutions showed bright fluorescence under the UV lamp before the addition of the sample solution (Fig. 10(a)(c)), and the fluorescence was basically quenched after the addition of the solution (Fig. 10(b)(d)). The F/F0 values of 0.11 and 0.19 for N-CDs and N, B-CDs, respectively, after the addition of the sample solution, also indicated that the fluorescence was quenched mainly. This suggests that real samples containing rutin components can also be used to detect N-CDs and N, B-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Random forest regression model predicts detection accuracy
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the main steps in constructing a Random Forest (RF) model include (i) collecting and organizing the original data. Perform necessary data preprocessing, including missing value filling, outlier handling, and feature scaling. (ii) Determine the number of decision trees in the forest, select the number of features to be considered in each split, and set the maximum depth of a single decision tree or other constraints, such as the minimum sample split (min_samples_split) and the minimum number of leaf node samples (min_samples_leaf), and decide whether or not to use self-sampling to create a training subset. (iii) For each tree, take samples from the original dataset in a relaxed manner to form a new training subset. Using the selected subset of features, select the optimal split features at each node. The decision tree is constructed recursively until a preset stopping condition is satisfied, As shown in Fig. 9(b). (iv) For each sample in the test set, pass it to each tree in the forest to get the respective prediction results. Average the predictions of all trees as the final prediction for that sample. (v) Measure the performance of the model on the test set using appropriate evaluation metrics (e.g., mean square error (MSE), root mean square error (RMSE), and coefficient of determination (R2)). (vi) Analyze the performance of the model and adjust the parameters or improve the model as needed. Through the above steps, the random forest regression model can provide good generalization ability and robustness while maintaining high accuracy.

Fig. 12 (a) and Fig. 13 (a) are obtained after the Savitzky-Golay filter (“SG smoothing”), which smoothes the data by fitting local polynomials while preserving key features of the data, such as peaks and trends. The Inter-sample Correlation Coefficient matrix is a statistical measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two or more samples. Fig. 12(b) and Fig. 12(b) show the correlation between spectral data at different concentrations. From left to right and top to bottom, each cell represents the correlation coefficient between the spectral data at two specific concentrations. The color coding reflects the magnitude of the correlation coefficient, with red indicating a positive correlation, blue indicating a negative correlation, and the shade of the color indicating the magnitude of the absolute value of the correlation coefficient. White color indicates that the correlation coefficient is close to 0, i.e., there is almost no correlation. The importance of features at different concentrations is demonstrated in Fig. 12(c) and Fig. 13(c), from which it can be seen that the importance of features is in descending order, with features being more critical to the predictive ability of the model at zero concentration. The plots in Fig. 12(d) and Fig. 13(d) demonstrate the relationship between predicted and actual concentrations. There are two sets of data in the plots; one is the exact expected values (blue dots), and the other is the ideal line (red line). From the results, it can be seen that there is a good linear relationship between the predicted and actual values, which indicates a high prediction accuracy of the model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by rutin
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism further, we investigated rutin's UV–visible absorption with and without CDs. The findings are displayed in Fig. 14 (a). The figure illustrates that rutin's UV–visible absorption spectrum features two absorption peaks in the 280–420 nm region (red line), and the addition of N-CDs (blue line) and N, B-CDs (green line) significantly reduced the UV–visible absorption. It can be preliminarily inferred that dynamic quenching occurred between rutin and N-CDs and N, B-CDs [57]. However, we also found that the absorption spectra of rutin, N-CDs, and N, B-CDs partially overlapped in the range of 280–420 nm by observing Fig. 14(a), and speculated that there might be a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism between rutin and N-CDs and N, B-CDs.

Due to the surface of N-CDs and N, B-CDs have diverse functional groups(Fig. 4(a)). The structural formula of rutin proves that it contains hydroxyl groups on its surface. If rutin is mixed with N-CDs and N, B-CDs, the two will react. The amine and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of N-CDs and N, B-CDs can interact with some of the hydroxyl groups of rutin through hydrogen bonding in addition, π-π interactions may also occur between N-CDs and N, B-CDs and the aromatic layer of rutin [33]. Through energy transfer, these two interactions cause a non-radiative emission process (Fig. 14(c)), which in turn causes the emission intensity to drop, causing fluorescence quenching of N-CDs and N, B-CDs (Fig. 14(e)). Fig. 14(d) and (f) Schematic diagrams of the ideal electron energy levels and electron-leap channels of N-CDs and N, B-CDs are constructed, respectively. It is clearly observed from the figures that their core electronic energy level structures are the same. After the addition of Rutin solution, the electrons in the excited state can jump to the unfilled orbitals of Rutin by nonradiative leaps using the photopump without returning to the ground state, thus suppressing the fluorescence emission of N-CDs and N, B-CDs. It is shown that the ultrafast photoelectron transfer process in the CDs + Rutin luminescent system leads to a dynamic burst of CDs fluorescence. At the same time, we found that when rutin was mixed with N-CDs or N, B-CDs separately, crystalline precipitates were produced. Therefore, between carbon dots and rutin, a static quenching also takes place. In summary, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of CDs in this study may be caused by a combination of static quenching, dynamic quenching, and FRET mechanism.

In the present study, using o-phenylenediamine, glucose, urea, and boron oxide as the raw ingredients, the hydrothermal technique was used to prepare both yellow luminescent N-CDs and blue-green luminescent N-B-CDs. Through the use of TEM, XRD, zeta potential, FT-IR, Raman, XPS, and PL fluorescence, the preparedN-CDs and N, B-CDs were characterized. The synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs were spherical, uniform in size, and well dispersed, according to the characterization results. Furthermore, the surface passivation of N-CDs prevents the emission peak position from changing with the excitation wavelength. In contrast, the emission peak position of N, B-CDs has a weak dependence on the excitation wavelength. By analyzing the band gap values of N-CDs and N, B-CDs, it is found that heteroatoms can effectively regulate the band gap and change the fluorescence properties. Synthesized N-CDs and N, B-CDs were utilized as fluorescent probes to detect rutin solution and investigate the reaction mechanism. The results showed that the addition of rutin solution caused the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of N-CDs and N, B-CDs. In the concentration range of 0–1.6 × 10−6 mol/mL and 0–1.4 × 10−6 mol/mL, respectively, the PL intensities of N-CDs and N, B-CDs demonstrated good linear relationships.

Furthermore, we used the random forest algorithm to predict the detection model. The results showed that the experimental value and the expected value were basically coincident, and the rapid detection of rutin could be realized by the fluorescence method. A careful analysis of the quenching mechanism demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs and N, B-CDs can be attributed to static quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms. This research offers a quick and easy approach to synthesizing doped carbon dots and detecting commercially available rutin.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51764052, 62063035), Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 202401AT070942, 202301AU070127), and Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in the University of Yunnan Province.

Analytical Chemistry, 2014, 86(15):7436-45. doi: 10.1021/ac501085d.
